model_summary: Compact, gVAR-ready initial observation model capturing six core criteria derived from the complaint (episodic tingling with anxiety and a reinforcing loop, catastrophic interpretation, medical uncertainty, and interoceptive monitoring) and eight modifiable predictors spanning cognitive-behavioral, mindfulness/interoceptive, sleep, social, and digital-solution domains. The plan uses high-variance momentary EMA (6 prompts/day for 14 days) plus a daily diary, keeps measures on 1–9 Likert-type scales to maximize within-person variance, and minimizes redundancy to support estimability and interpretable lagged effects. A full predictor–criterion relevance grid is provided to seed gVAR parameterization, with conservative assumptions where effects are nonlinear or uncertain.
variable_selection_notes: Rationale: The six criteria (C01–C06) cover the somatic sensation, immediate affective response, reinforcing feedback loop, threat interpretation, medical uncertainty, and heightened monitoring—capturing the core dynamics described in the complaint. The eight predictors (P01–P08) target cognitive reappraisal, interoceptive mindfulness, medical-communication clarity, paced breathing, behavioral activation, social support, sleep regularity, and digital-tool engagement. This set aims to maximize within-person variance while limiting redundancy across self-regulation constructs. Potential biases: self-report measures may inflate perceived threat during episodes; mitigate via brief, standardized prompts and explicit anchors; missingness risk is moderated by reminders and user-friendly interfaces.

momentary_ema.notes: Distribute prompts across waking hours with mild jitter to reduce predictability; ensure prompts do not intrude during sleep.

daily_diary.notes: Evening diary for sleep quality and day-context; optional add-ons if user is willing.

passive_streams.notes: Not implemented in this initial design.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: All chosen variables are measurable with standard smartphone-based EMA; plan data-quality checks and imputation for sporadic missingness.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Expect substantial within-person variability due to episodic tingling and arousal; plan z-scoring per participant and per-variable to stabilize cross-person comparisons.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Moderate risk due to participant burden; mitigate with reminders, brief prompts, and optional skip with auto-fill options.

safety_notes: Not a diagnosis. No medication dosing or medical procedures advised. If new acute risk signs emerge (self-harm, mania, severe deterioration), seek professional care and implement safety protocol.

criteria_variables.label: Episodic tingling in hands/feet

measurement.item_or_signal: In the moment, rate your tingling in hands/feet on a 1–9 scale (1 = none, 9 = severe).

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Best match: paresthesias_distal_extremities. Variable C01 describes episodic tingling in hands/feet measured in the moment on a 1–9 EMA scale. The leaf paresthesias_distal_extremities directly encodes distal extremity tingling/paresthesias, which is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this symptom. Mechanistically, it captures a somatic sensory symptom consistent with interoceptive monitoring and threat interpretations described in the pseudoprofile; the measurement context (EMA self-report, smartphone prompts, 1–9 scale, 6 prompts/day) aligns with capturing within-person fluctuations of this distal sensory symptom.

predictor_variables.label: Threat reinterpretation via cognitive reappraisal (CBT) usage
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Cognitive reappraisal reduces threat appraisal and arousal, facilitating decoupling of anxiety from somatic sensations.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Brief cognitive-reframing prompts; structured thought records; in-the-mMoment app cues

measurement.item_or_signal: To what extent are you using cognitive reappraisal to view the tingling as less dangerous? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Increases threat reappraisal, reduces catastrophizing about sensations.

feasibility.notes: CBT-relevant items are brief and scalable.

(1-9)” and the EMA_self_report modality. It sits under cognitive_restructuring and emotion_linked_reappraisal, consistent with decoupling threat from somatic sensations, which matches the described mechanism. Other candidates are broader or refer to related but distinct constructs (e.g., safety reframing, uncertainty tolerance) and thus are less specific to threat appraisal reframing as used here.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the moment, rate your tingling in hands/feet on a 1–9 scale (1 = none, 9 = severe).

criteria.label: Acute anxiety when sensations occur
criteria.query_text_used: Acute anxiety when sensations occur | Operational item/signal: Right now, when tingling occurs, rate your anxiety level on a 1–9 scale (1 = not anxious, 9 = extremely anxious). | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Acute anxiety when sensations occur' corresponds to the item 'Right now, when tingling occurs, rate your anxiety level on a 1–9 scale'. The closest CRITERION leaf is 27096 (alarm_response_to_normal_bodily_signs) under somatic symptom/illness anxiety domains, capturing an acute anxiety response to bodily cues. This provides the most specific operational match for momentary interoceptive anxiety measured via EMA. In the pseudoprofile, this aligns with core somatic/interoceptive dynamics (interoceptive monitoring, threat interpretation) and maps to a concrete, var_item–level construct rather than broader phobic or panic-state categories.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, when tingling occurs, rate your anxiety level on a 1–9 scale (1 = not anxious, 9 = extremely anxious).

criteria.label: Symptom–anxiety amplification loop
criteria.query_text_used: Symptom–anxiety amplification loop | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did anxiety amplify the tingling sensation (1–9)? | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Symptom–anxiety amplification loop' measured via EMA: 'Since the last prompt, to what extent did anxiety amplify the tingling sensation (1–9)?' This maps to a mechanism where anxiety magnifies bodily sensations. The closest operational leaf is 'alarm_response_to_normal_bodily_signs' (illness anxiety disorder branch) which encodes alarm/activation in response to normal bodily cues, i.e., an amplification loop between somatic symptoms and anxiety.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how dangerous or threatening do you consider the tingling? (1 = not dangerous at all, 9 = extremely dangerous).

criteria.label: Uncertainty about medical cause
criteria.query_text_used: Uncertainty about medical cause | Operational item/signal: Rate your uncertainty about the medical cause of tingling (1 = completely certain, 9 = completely uncertain). | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Uncertainty about medical cause' maps to a cognitive health-anxiety construct. The measurement item rates uncertainty about the medical cause of tingling on a 1-9 scale via momentary EMA. The closest, most specific CRITERION leaf is 'illness anxiety disorder (hypochondriasis) / misinterpretation_of_bodily_sensations', which captures misinterpretation of bodily cues and concern about medical illness. This aligns with the pseudoprofile's 'medical uncertainty' predictor and interoceptive monitoring dynamics without overfitting to a single clinical diagnosis.

measurement.item_or_signal: Rate your uncertainty about the medical cause of tingling (1 = completely certain, 9 = completely uncertain).

criteria.label: Heightened bodily monitoring/interoceptive focus
criteria.query_text_used: Heightened bodily monitoring/interoceptive focus | Operational item/signal: Right now, to what extent are you actively monitoring your body sensations? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely). | Polarity: higher_is_worse

mapping.rationale: Variable label Heightened bodily monitoring/interoceptive focus maps to a hypervigilance construct. Measurement item 'Right now, to what extent are you actively monitoring your body sensations?' on a 1–9 EMA scale directly captures interoceptive monitoring intensity. Best-matching leaf is CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / somatic symptom and related disorders / somatic symptom disorder / hypervigilance_to_body_sensations. Mechanism: heightened interoceptive focus increases attention to bodily cues, amplifying perceived threat and sustaining anxiety via a reinforcing loop, which aligns with the core dynamics described in the pseudoprofile. This mapping uses the most specific operational construct available among candidates without overfitting to a narrow subtype.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, to what extent are you actively monitoring your body sensations? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely).

predictors.label: Threat reinterpretation via cognitive reappraisal (CBT) usage
predictors.query_text_used: Threat reinterpretation via cognitive reappraisal (CBT) usage | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Cognitive reappraisal reduces threat appraisal and arousal, facilitating decoupling of anxiety from somatic sensations. | Intervention examples: Brief cognitive-reframing prompts; structured thought records; in-the-mMoment app cues | Measurement: EMA_self_report | To what extent are you using cognitive reappraisal to view the tingling as less dangerous? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies > Behavior_Change_and_Activation

mapping.rationale: The variable represents Threat reinterpretation via cognitive reappraisal usage within CBT. The best-matching leaf is 4162: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / emotion_linked_reappraisal / threat_and_anxiety / threat_appraisal_reframe. This leaf exactly encodes threat appraisal reframing as a cognitive-behavioral restructuring mechanism, aligning with the measurement item “To what extent are you using cognitive reappraisal to view the tingling as less dangerous? (1-9)” and the EMA_self_report modality. It sits under cognitive_restructuring and emotion_linked_reappraisal, consistent with decoupling threat from somatic sensations, which matches the described mechanism. Other candidates are broader or refer to related but distinct constructs (e.g., safety reframing, uncertainty tolerance) and thus are less specific to threat appraisal reframing as used here.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / emotion_linked_reappraisal / threat_and_anxiety / threat_appraisal_reframe

source_variable.label: Threat reinterpretation via cognitive reappraisal (CBT) usage
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Cognitive reappraisal reduces threat appraisal and arousal, facilitating decoupling of anxiety from somatic sensations.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief cognitive-reframing prompts; structured thought records; in-the-mMoment app cues

measurement.item_or_signal: To what extent are you using cognitive reappraisal to view the tingling as less dangerous? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Increases threat reappraisal, reduces catastrophizing about sensations.

feasibility.notes: CBT-relevant items are brief and scalable.

predictors.label: Interoceptive mindfulness engagement
predictors.query_text_used: Interoceptive mindfulness engagement | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Mindfulness reduces interoceptive hypervigilance and improves nonjudgmental body awareness. | Intervention examples: Brief body-scan, mindful breathing, 3-minute interoception prompts | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Right now, to what extent are you practicing body-focused mindfulness or interoceptive awareness? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Third_Wave_and_Contextual_Therapies > Mindfulness_Based_Interventions

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: interoceptive_awareness_practice under Mindfulness_Based_Interventions (third_wave_contextual_therapies > body_based_mindfulness). This directly corresponds to the variable label 'Interoceptive mindfulness engagement' and to the EMA item 'Right now, to what extent are you practicing body-focused mindfulness or interoceptive awareness? (1-9)' measured via momentary smartphone EMA. Mechanism rationale aligns with the leaf's construct: mindfulness reduces interoceptive hypervigilance and improves nonjudgmental body awareness, which matches the intended intervention target. Among candidates, this leaf provides the most specific, item-aligned mapping for interoceptive mindfulness engagement within the third-wave contextual mindfulness framework.

source_variable.label: Interoceptive mindfulness engagement
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Mindfulness reduces interoceptive hypervigilance and improves nonjudgmental body awareness.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief body-scan, mindful breathing, 3-minute interoception prompts

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, to what extent are you practicing body-focused mindfulness or interoceptive awareness? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Increased interoceptive awareness supports nonreactivity, reducing acute anxiety.

feasibility.notes: Mindfulness prompts are brief and well-tolerated.

predictors.label: Structured medical communication clarity
predictors.query_text_used: Structured medical communication clarity | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Clear medical communication reduces uncertainty and threat about etiologies. | Intervention examples: Scheduled brief patient-provider questions; summary handouts; follow-up video explainer | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, how clear did medical information feel about the tingling cause? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Foundations_and_Common_Factors > Psychoeducation_Modules > Clarity_of_medical_information

mapping.rationale: Best-leaf: 'informed_consent_clarity' (PSYCHO / foundations_and_common_factors / ethics_consent_and_safety) aligns with 'Structured medical communication clarity' as a Psychoeducation/ethics construct focused on clarity of medical information. The measurement item asks: 'Today, how clear did medical information feel about the tingling cause?'—which maps directly to the clarity of medical information. Mechanism: clearer medical information reduces uncertainty and threat about etiologies, consistent with the stated mechanism rationale for the variable and the predictive role of information clarity in interoceptive threat and monitoring.

source_variable.label: Structured medical communication clarity
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Clear medical communication reduces uncertainty and threat about etiologies.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Scheduled brief patient-provider questions; summary handouts; follow-up video explainer

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, how clear did medical information feel about the tingling cause? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Better clarity reduces medical uncertainty and threat appraisal.

feasibility.notes: Provider communication prompts are feasible with simple UI.

predictors.label: Diaphragmatic breathing and paced respiration
predictors.query_text_used: Diaphragmatic breathing and paced respiration | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Breathing regulation reduces arousal and informs interoceptive processing. | Intervention examples: 3-minute paced-breathing, diaphragmatic breathing before/after sensations | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Right now, are you using paced breathing or diaphragmatic breathing? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration > Breathing_and_ARousal_Management

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Diaphragmatic breathing and paced respiration' maps closest to the leaf 'diaphragmatic_breathing_practice' (PSYCHO > breath_and_relaxation_skills). This leaf represents diaphragmatic breathing as a specific, operational breathing technique used to downregulate arousal, aligning with the measurement item 'Right now, are you using paced breathing or diaphragmatic breathing?' and the mechanism of interoceptive/arousal regulation. It is more specific than broader paced breathing options and directly matches the diaphragmatic component of the variable.

source_variable.label: Diaphragmatic breathing and paced respiration
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Breathing regulation reduces arousal and informs interoceptive processing.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, are you using paced breathing or diaphragmatic breathing? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Breathing regulation lowers momentary anxiety and arousal.

feasibility.notes: Breathing prompts are quick and safe.

predictors.label: Behavioral activation and reduction of bodily monitoring
predictors.query_text_used: Behavioral activation and reduction of bodily monitoring | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Engagement in non-somatic activities reduces focus on bodily cues and disrupts the amplification loop. | Intervention examples: Plan 1 short activity per day; schedule non-body-related tasks | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, have you engaged in an activity away from body monitoring? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Movement_Physical_Function_and_Somatic_Care > Behavioral_Activation_and_Engagement

mapping.rationale: Best match for var P05: 'Behavioral activation and reduction of bodily monitoring' maps to activation planning and engagement. The candidate leaf 4111 is 'behavioral_activity_planning_system' under PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / behavior_change_and_activation / planning_and_grading / activation_design, which directly targets activation design and behavioral planning. The variable’s measurement item asks about engaging in an activity away from body monitoring (1-9, EMA, 6 prompts/day), aligning with activation and engagement. Mechanistically, engagement in non-somatic activities reduces focus on bodily cues and disrupts the amplification loop, as described in the mechanism_rationale.

source_variable.label: Behavioral activation and reduction of bodily monitoring
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Engagement in non-somatic activities reduces focus on bodily cues and disrupts the amplification loop.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Plan 1 short activity per day; schedule non-body-related tasks

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, have you engaged in an activity away from body monitoring? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Active engagement distracts from interoceptive focus and reduces anxiety.

feasibility.notes: Easy to implement with daily prompts.

predictors.label: Social support and sense of belonging
predictors.query_text_used: Social support and sense of belonging | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Perceived social support buffers anxiety and reduces vigilance toward sensations. | Intervention examples: Brief check-in prompts with a friend/family; supportive text; group-related activities | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Right now, how supported do you feel by your social network? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Social_Support_and_Belonging > Belonging_and_Support

mapping.rationale: Variable P06 targets social support and sense of belonging. The measurement item asks about current perceived support from the social network on a 1–9 Likert scale via EMA prompts (6 per day). The leaf 1382 maps to SOCIAL > social_support_and_belonging > belonging_isolation_and_meaning > safety_and_trust_in_support > boundary_setting_with_support_network, which best matches belonging, perceived support, and trust in the social network, aligning with the intended construct and measurement method.

source_variable.label: Social support and sense of belonging
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Perceived social support buffers anxiety and reduces vigilance toward sensations.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief check-in prompts with a friend/family; supportive text; group-related activities

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how supported do you feel by your social network? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Perceived support reduces anxiety reactivity to sensations.

feasibility.notes: Social prompts may depend on participants' networks.

predictors.label: Sleep optimization and circadian regularity
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep optimization and circadian regularity | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Sleep quality and circadian stability influence arousal and interoceptive sensitivity. | Intervention examples: Regular sleep-wake schedule; limit late-night screen time | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Last night’s sleep quality (1-9) and wake time regularity today (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration > Circadian_Entrainment

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: BIO / medical_assessment_and_physiology_testing / sleep_and_respiratory_testing / circadian_and_sleep_tracking / sleep_diary. The variable Sleep optimization and circadian regularity uses EMA with last night’s sleep quality (1-9) and wake time regularity today (1-9). The sleep_diary leaf explicitly captures nightly sleep quality and wake-time regularity, aligning with the measurement items and the circadian entrainment focus. Mechanism rationale: Sleep quality and circadian stability influence arousal and interoceptive sensitivity, fitting the described interoceptive monitoring and anxiety loop. Context is supportive rather than overfitted to the pseudoprofile specifics.

source_variable.label: Sleep optimization and circadian regularity
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Sleep quality and circadian stability influence arousal and interoceptive sensitivity.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Regular sleep-wake schedule; limit late-night screen time

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night’s sleep quality (1-9) and wake time regularity today (1-9).

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces daytime anxiety and interoceptive sensitivity.

feasibility.notes: Sleep questions feasible; may require daily diary input.

predictors.label: Digital tools usage for well-being (Care_Delivery_Formats)
predictors.query_text_used: Digital tools usage for well-being (Care_Delivery_Formats) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Use of digital tools can support CBT, mindfulness, and tracking; acts as an enabler of salutogenic routines. | Intervention examples: Using a mood-tracking orBreathing app; guided prompts; online psychoeducation modules | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Are you actively using a digital tool for well-being right now? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Digital_and_Tech_Enabled_Solutions > Digital_Tools_and_Supports

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is mood_and_context_tracking_tool (PSYCHO / digital_and_tech_enabled_solutions / digital_tools_and_supports). The variable P08 describes digital tools usage for well-being with EMA self-report via smartphone; the measurement item asks if a digital tool is actively being used for well-being at the moment. A mood/context tracking tool directly captures real-time digital tool usage for well-being and aligns with the measurement mode (EMA_self_report) and device (smartphone app). This is more specific to active tool usage in the moment than broader CBT/workbooks or modules, making it the most specific operational construct among candidates.

source_variable.label: Digital tools usage for well-being (Care_Delivery_Formats)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Use of digital tools can support CBT, mindfulness, and tracking; acts as an enabler of salutogenic routines.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Using a mood-tracking orBreathing app; guided prompts; online psychoeducation modules

measurement.item_or_signal: Are you actively using a digital tool for well-being right now? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Digital tools can support coping strategies that reduce anxiety.

feasibility.notes: Digital-tools usage varies; track both exposure and engagement.
